name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّمَّا يَعْبُدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ مَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَمَا يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُهُم مِّن قَبْلُ ۚ وَإِنَّا لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ غَيْرَ مَنقُوصٍ (١٠٩)
```

```english
So do not be in doubt about what these worship: they worship just as their fathers worshiped before, and We shall surely pay them their full share, undiminished. (109)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (١١٠)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose about it, and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord, a decision would have been made between them; indeed they are in grave doubt concerning it. (110)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ كُلًّا لَّمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١١١)
```

```english
Your Lord will indeed recompense everyone fully for their works. Indeed, He is well aware of what they do. (111)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ وَمَن تَابَ مَعَكَ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١٢)
```

```english
So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not overstep the bounds. Indeed, He watches what you do. (112)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَرْكَنُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ فَتَمَسَّكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (١١٣)
```

```english
Do not incline toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you, and you will not have any friend besides Allah; then you will not be helped. (113)
```

```arabic
وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ طَرَفَىِ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَزُلَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ يُذْهِبْنَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ذِكْرَىٰ لِلذَّٰكِرِينَ (١١٤)
```

```english
Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night. Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds. That is an admonition for the mindful. (114)
```

```arabic
وَٱصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١١٥)
```

```english
And be patient; indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous. (115)
```

```arabic
فَلَوْلَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ أُو۟لُوا۟ بَقِيَّةٍ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْفَسَادِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّنْ أَنجَيْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَآ أُتْرِفُوا۟ فِيهِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (١١٦)
```

```english
Why were there not among the generations before you a remnant [of the wise] who might forbid corruption in the earth, except a few of those whom We delivered from among them? Those who were wrongdoers pursued that in which they had been granted affluence, and they were a guilty lot. (116)
```

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُهَا مُصْلِحُونَ (١١٧)
```

```english
Your Lord would never destroy the townships unjustly while their inhabitants were bringing about reform. (117)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً ۖ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ (١١٨)
```

```english
Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community; but they continue to differ, (118)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ ۚ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ ۗ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (١١٩)
```

```english
except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and that is why He created them—and the word of your Lord has been fulfilled: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together!’ (119)
```

## (Commentary)

When the Exalted detailed for His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) the stories of past nations (الأمم الغابرة), and what their polytheism (الشرك), transgression (الفسق), denial of Allah’s signs, and arrogance (الاستكبار) in refusing the truth to which their prophets called them led to—how it drove them to destruction and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), and then to the eternal punishment of the Fire on a day for which mankind will be gathered—He then summarized the discourse regarding their affair in the preceding verses.

In these verses, He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he and those with him should take heed (الاعتبار) from this, and be certain that polytheism (الشرك) in Allah and corruption (الفساد) on earth lead man only to destruction and ruin. Therefore, they must adhere to the path of servitude (العبودية), hold fast to patience (الصبر) and prayer (الصلاة), and not lean toward those who do wrong (الذين ظلموا), lest the Fire touch them, for they would have no protectors (أولياء) besides Allah, and then they would not be helped. They must know that the word of Allah is the highest, and the word of those who disbelieve is the lowest, and that the respite Allah grants them is only to fulfill the word of truth that proceeded from Him, which shall be completed by His recompensing them on the Day of Resurrection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِمَّا يَعْبُدُ هؤُلاءِ ما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُهُمْ** — **So do not be in doubt about what these [people] worship: they do not worship [anything] except as their fathers worshipped [before]** (11:109) etc.

This is a derivation from the preceding detail of the stories of past nations who wronged themselves by taking partners [with Allah] and spreading corruption on earth, whereupon Allah seized them with punishment. Those referred to by His saying, "these" (هؤُلاءِ), are the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). His saying, "they do not worship [anything] except as their fathers worshipped" (ما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُهُمْ), means they worship them out of blind imitation (التقليد) just like their fathers; thus, the later ones follow the path trodden by the earlier ones without any proof (حجة). What is meant by "their share" (نصيبهم) is their lot in exchange for their polytheism and transgression.

His saying, "undiminished" (غَيْرَ مَنْقُوصٍ), is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "the share," and it contains an emphasis of His saying, "We will surely pay them their full [due]" (لَمُوَفُّوهُمْ), for "paying in full" (التوفية) is the rendering of another's right in its entirety and perfection. It also serves to make the disbelievers despair of divine pardon.

The meaning of the verse is: If you have heard the stories of the ancients—that they used to worship gods besides Allah and deny His signs—and you have known the sunnah (السنة) — *the established divine way* — of Allah the Exalted regarding them, which is destruction in this world and the destination of eternal Fire in the Hereafter, then do not be in any doubt (شك) or hesitation (مرية) regarding the worship of these people, who are your folk. They worship only as their fathers worshipped, based on blind imitation without proof or clear evidence (بينة). We shall surely give them their share of the recompense for their deeds without diminishing any of it through intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — or pardon, whatever it may be.

It is possible that what is meant by "their fathers" (آباؤُهُمْ) is the past destroyed nations, rather than the ancestors of the Arabs after Ishmael, for example. This is because Allah called them their "forefathers" (آباءهم الأولين), as in His saying: **أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ أَمْ جاءَهُمْ ما لَمْ يَأْتِ آباءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** — **Have they not pondered the word, or has there come to them what did not come to their forefathers?** (23:68). This is more appropriate and better. The meaning—based on this—would be: Do not be in doubt about the worship of your people; these people worship only like the worship of those destroyed nations who are their "fathers," and there is no doubt that We shall give them their lot of recompense just as We did with their fathers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ فَاخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ** — **Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it; and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them; and they are surely in a skeptical doubt regarding it.** (11:110). Since these verses are intended for taking heed (الاعتبار) from the stories mentioned in the Surah, and the stories themselves were narrated so that the people might be admonished and judge with truth regarding their taking partners for Allah, their denial of Allah’s signs, and their accusing the Quran of being a fabrication against Allah the Exalted.

In these verses—intended for taking heed—He addressed the matter of their taking gods and their denial of the Quran. Allah mentioned that the people's worship of partners is like the worship of their predecessors from the past nations, and punishment will reach them just as it reached their predecessors. Furthermore, their disagreement concerning the Book of Allah is like the disagreement of the nation of Moses (عليه السلام) regarding the Book Allah gave him, and Allah will judge between them concerning that wherein they differed. Thus, His saying, "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ فَاخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ), is an allusion to the Jews' disagreement regarding the Torah after Moses.

His saying, "and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them" (وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ). The Glorified has repeatedly mentioned in His Book that people's differences in worldly matters are something they are disposed toward by nature (فطروا عليها), but their differences in religious matters have no source other than out of envy (البغي) after knowledge has come to them. The Exalted said: **وَما كانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا** — **Mankind were but a single community; then they differed.** (10:19). And He said: **وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعلمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ** — **Those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves.** (3:19). And He said: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ** — **Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. And none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves.** (2:213).

Allah the Glorified has decreed that He will pay people the reward for what they have done and the recompense for what they have earned. This would necessitate that He judge between them concerning that wherein they differed at the very moment they differed. However, the Exalted decreed another decree: that He would grant them enjoyment on earth until the Day of Resurrection so that they might populate the world and earn in their worldly life for their Hereafter, as He said: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ** — **There will be for you on the earth an abode and a livelihood for a while.** (2:36). The requirement of these two decrees is to delay the judgment between those who differ in the religion of Allah and His Book out of envy until the Day of Resurrection.

If you ask: Why then did Allah destroy the past nations for their wrongdoing? Why did He not delay them until the Day of Resurrection because of a prior decree from Him?

I say: The source of their destruction was not their disbelief (الكفر) nor their sins—in short, not merely their disagreement in religious matters—such that the other decree would oppose it and delay it until the Day of Resurrection. Rather, it was a third decree alluded to by the saying of the Glorified: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** — **There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged.** (10:47).

In summary, His saying, "and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them" (وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ), indicates that people's disagreement regarding the Book is the meeting point of two decrees from Allah the Glorified: one necessitates judging between people in what they differed, and the other necessitates that Allah grant them enjoyment until the Day of Resurrection and not recompense them for their deeds [immediately]. The requirement of all this is that their punishment be delayed until the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, "and they are surely in a skeptical doubt regarding it" (وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ). "Inducing doubt" (الإرابة) is the casting of doubt into the heart. Thus, describing "doubt" (الشك) as "skeptical/inducing doubt" (المريب) is like the expressions "a lasting shade" (ظِلًّا ظَلِيلاً), "a concealed veil" (حِجاباً مَسْتُوراً), and "a forbidden ban" (حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً), etc., and it serves to emphasize the meaning of doubt.

It appears that the referent of the pronoun in His saying, "and they" (وَإِنَّهُمْ), is the nation of Moses, namely the Jews. It is right for them to doubt it, for the chain of transmission (سند) of the current Torah ends with what was written for them by a man from their priests named Ezra when they intended to return from Babylon to the Holy Land after the end of the captivity. The Torah had been burned years before that during the burning of the Temple. Thus, the termination of its chain of transmission at a single individual naturally necessitates doubt (الريب) regarding it. The Gospel is similar to it in terms of its chain of transmission.

Furthermore, the current Torah contains things that the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — does not accept attributing to a heavenly book, which necessitates doubt regarding it.

As for returning the pronoun in His saying, "and they" (وَإِنَّهُمْ), to the polytheist Arabs, and in His saying, "regarding it" (مِنْهُ), to the Quran—as some commentators have done—it is far from correct. This is because Allah the Glorified had already completed the proof (الحجة) against them at the beginning of the Surah that it is His Book revealed from Him to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and He challenged them with the like of His saying: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ** — **Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated’** (11:13). There is no meaning, alongside that, in attributing doubt to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ كُلًّا لَمَّا لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمالَهُمْ إِنَّهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ** — **Indeed, your Lord will surely pay each of them in full for their deeds. Indeed He is well aware of what they do.** (11:111). The word *inna* (إن) is the particle resembling a verb, and its noun is His saying, "each" (كُلًّا), which is nunated and detached from its genitive construction (مقطوعا عن الإضافة); the estimation is "all of them" (كلهم), meaning those who differed. Its predicate (خبر) is His saying, "will surely pay them in full" (لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ), where the *lam* and the *nun* serve to emphasize the predicate. His saying, *lammā* (لَمَّا), is composed of a *lam* indicating an oath and a doubled *ma* that separates the two *lams* and provides further emphasis. The response to the oath (جواب القسم) is omitted, indicated by the predicate of *inna*.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Indeed, each of these who differed, I swear, your Lord will surely pay them in full and give them [the recompense of] their deeds—that is, their reward—for He is well aware (خبير) of the deeds of good and evil they perform.

It is reported in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* from Abu Hayyan, from Ibn al-Hajib, that *lammā* (لَمَّا) in the verse is the jussive particle (لما الجازمة), and the omission of its governed word is common in usage; one says, "I went out and *lammā*" (خرجت ولما), and "I traveled and *lammā*" (سافرت ولما) [meaning: and have not yet returned/arrived]. He then said: It is better, based on this, to estimate: "they have not yet been paid in full" (لما يوفوها); meaning: Indeed, each of them, since they have not yet been paid in full for their deeds, your Lord will surely pay them in full for them. This is a plausible view.

The people of exegesis have long literary discussions regarding the vocabulary and syntax of the verse, but we are not concerned with more than what we have presented. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the extensive commentaries.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَمَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ وَلا تَطْغَوْا إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ** — **So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not transgress. Indeed He watches what you do.** (11:112). It is said: "he stood" (قام), "he was firm" (ثبت), and "he was fixed" (ركز) with the same meaning, as mentioned by al-Raghib and others. It appears that the original sense taken for this is the standing (قيام) of a human being. This is because a human being, in all states and positions other than standing—such as sitting, lying down, kneeling, reclining, or prostrating—is not capable of all the actions he intends, such as grasping, extending, taking, returning, and everything else over which man is naturally dominant. But when he stands on his legs, he is in his most balanced state, which gives him mastery over all his actions—firmness, movement, taking, returning, giving, withholding, attracting, and repelling. He remains dominant over the faculties he possesses and their actions. Thus, the standing of a human represents his human personality with all its affairs.

Then it was used metaphorically for everything in its most balanced state, through which it masters its effects and actions. The "standing" (قيام) of a pillar is its being fixed upright; the "standing" of a tree is its being rooted on its trunk with its base firmly in the ground; the "standing" of a vessel containing liquid is its standing on its base so that its contents are not spilled; the "standing" of justice is its being spread over the land; and the "standing" of a sunnah or law is its being implemented in the land.

**Establishing** (الإقامة) is making a thing "stand" (قائماً), meaning making it in a state where all its effects follow such that it lacks none of them—like establishing justice, establishing the sunnah, establishing prayer (إقامة الصلاة), establishing testimony, establishing the prescribed punishments (إقامة الحدود), establishing the religion (إقامة الدين), and the like.

**Steadfastness** (الاستقامة) is the seeking of "standing" (القيام) from a thing and the demand for the appearance of all its effects and benefits. Thus, the steadfastness of a path is its being characterized by what is intended from a path, such as being straight, clear, and not misleading those who travel it. The steadfastness of a human being in a matter is his seeking from himself to stand by it and rectify it such that no corruption or deficiency enters it, and it comes out complete and perfect. The Exalted said: **قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا إِلَيْهِ** — **Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So be steadfast toward Him’** (41:6), meaning: stand by the right of His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in His divinity. And He said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا** — **Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast** (41:30), meaning: they remained firm in what they said in all affairs of their lives, leaning in their beliefs, ethics, and deeds only toward what accords with and suits monotheism; that is, they observe and preserve it in everything they face internally and externally. And He said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً** — **So set your face to the religion as a Hanif** (30:30), for what is meant by "setting the face" (إقامة الوجه) is the soul's setting itself from the direction it receives and faces the action. A human being's setting himself in a matter is his steadfastness (استقامته) in it; so understand.

Thus, His saying, "So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded" (فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ), means: be firm upon the religion, fulfilling its right according to how you were commanded to be steadfast. He was commanded to do so in His saying: **وَأَنْ أَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً وَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **And: ‘Set your face to the religion as a Hanif, and never be one of the polytheists’** (10:105), and His saying: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — **So set your face to the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has instilled in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know.** (30:30).

It is said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: He commanded His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with steadfastness like the steadfastness he was commanded with. This necessitates his being commanded (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by another revelation, even if not recited, as said by several [scholars]. It appears that this is a command for the continuation (الدوام) of steadfastness, which is adhering to the straight path (المنهج المستقيم) — the middle way between excess (الإفراط) and negligence (التفريط). It is a comprehensive word for everything related to knowledge, action, and all ethics. [End of quote].

As for his suggestion that there might be another revelation, not recited, alluded to by His saying, "just as you have been commanded" (كَما أُمِرْتَ)—meaning: be steadfast as you were previously commanded to be steadfast—this is far from the method of the Quran. Far be it that the Quranic exposition should rely on an unknown matter or a hidden, unmentioned principle. You have already known that the allusion therein is to what Allah commanded him regarding setting his face to the religion as a *Hanif*. Setting the face to the religion is steadfastness in the religion. The saying, "So set your face to the religion" (فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ), has appeared—as you know—in two Surahs, both of which are Meccan. Surah Yunus, which is one of them, was certainly revealed before this Surah, even if that is not conceded regarding the other Surah, which is Surah al-Rum.

As for his saying: "What is meant by His saying, 'So be steadfast' (فَاسْتَقِمْ), is the continuation of steadfastness, which is adhering to the straight path between excess and negligence," you have already known that the meaning of a human being's steadfastness in a matter is his firmness in preserving it and fulfilling its right in its entirety and perfection. A human being's steadfastness, in an absolute sense, is his rootedness and firmness for the duties that come upon him with all his strength and pillars, such that he leaves nothing of his power and ability for a deviation (غي) that has no effect.

If the intent of the command for steadfastness were the command to adhere to moderation (الاعتدال) between excess and negligence, it would have been more appropriate to follow this command with a prohibition of both excess and negligence together. Yet, the Exalted followed it with His saying, "and do not transgress" (وَلا تَطْغَوْا), thus prohibiting only excess (الإفراط). This is in the position of an explanatory apposition (عطف التفسير) for His saying, "So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you." This is the best evidence that what is meant by His saying, "So be steadfast," etc., is the command to manifest firmness in servitude (العبودية) and the necessity of standing by its right. Then He prohibited exceeding this limit and being arrogant against submission to Allah, thereby exiting the garb of servitude; thus it was said: "and do not transgress" (وَلا تَطْغَوْا), just as the past nations did. They were not afflicted except by excess rather than negligence, and by arrogance rather than self-abasement.

His saying, "and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you" (وَمَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ), is conjoined to the latent pronoun in "be steadfast" (فَاسْتَقِمْ); meaning: be steadfast, you and whoever has turned with you—that is, be steadfast all of you. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was singled out from among them and mentioned individually with them only to honor the station of Prophethood. Upon this His sunnah (السنة) proceeds in His speech, such as His saying: **آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — **The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well].** (2:285), and His saying: **يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ** — **the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him.** (66:8).

Furthermore, the command by which His saying, "So be steadfast," was restricted—namely His saying, "just as you have been commanded" (كَما أُمِرْتَ)—is specific to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and no one else shares it with him. For what was mentioned, such as His saying, "So set your face to the religion," etc., is specific to him. If it were said, "So be [all of you] steadfast" (فاستقيموا), it would not have been correct to restrict it by the previous command.

What is meant by "whoever has turned [to Allah] with the Prophet" are the believers who returned to Allah through faith (الإيمان). The application of "repentance" (التوبة) to the origin of faith—which is a return from polytheism—is frequently mentioned in the Quran, such as His saying: **وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ** — **and plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord! You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge. So forgive those who repent and follow Your way’** (40:7), among others.

His saying, "and do not transgress" (وَلا تَطْغَوْا), means: do not exceed your limit which primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation have drawn for you, which is servitude (العبودية) to Allah alone, just as those before you exceeded it, which led them to polytheism and drove them to destruction. It appears that "transgression" (الطغيان) in this sense is derived from "the water transgressed" (طغى الماء) when it exceeded its limit. Then it was used metaphorically for this spiritual matter, which is man's transgression in his life, due to the similarity in the effect, which is corruption (الفساد).

His saying, "Indeed He watches what you do" (إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ), is a justification for the content of what preceded it. The meaning of the verse is: remain firm upon the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) and adhere to the path of servitude without wavering or vacillation, and let those who believe with you remain firm. Do not exceed the limit set for you, for Allah is watchful (بصير) over what you do, and He will take you to account if you oppose His command.

In this noble verse, there is a tone of severity (التشديد) that is not hidden; one does not sense in it any traces of mercy or signs of gentleness. It was preceded by verses containing the account of taking the past nations and bygone generations to task for their deeds, and Allah the Glorified’s lack of need for them—matters at which souls are stunned and hearts take flight.

This is aside from what is in His saying, "So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded," in terms of singling out the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for mention and separating him from among the believers to honor his station. However, that serves to indicate the reaching of the peak of severity in his regard. For his prior specification for mention necessitates that the terror of the address and the awe of the speech from the station of Might and Grandeur be directed toward him alone, equivalent to what is directed toward the entire nation until the Day of Resurrection. This is similar to the severity in His saying: **وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ** — **Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly leaned toward them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death.** (17:74-75).

For this reason, most commentators have mentioned that his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying, "Surah Hud has made me white-haired," refers to this verse. The discussion regarding this will come in the forthcoming traditional discourse (البحث الروائي), Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ** — **Do not lean toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you, and you will have no protectors besides Allah, then you will not be helped.** (11:113). It is said in *al-Sihah*: "he leaned toward him" (ركن إليه) like *nasara*, "leaning" (ركونا): he inclined and found rest. The "pillar" (الركن), with a *damma*, is the stronger side, the great matter, power, and protection. [End of quote]. Something similar is in *Lisan al-Arab*, and in *al-Misbah* it says that "leaning" (الركون) is relying (الاعتماد) on a thing.

Al-Raghib said: The "pillar" (ركن) of a thing is its side toward which one finds rest, and it is used metaphorically for strength. The Exalted said: **لَوْ أَنَّ لِي بِكُمْ قُوَّةً أَوْ آوِي إِلى رُكْنٍ شَدِيدٍ** — **[He said,] ‘If only I had the power to [overpower] you, or could take refuge in a powerful support!’** (11:80). And "I leaned toward so-and-so" (ركنت إلى فلان) is *arkanu* with a *fatha*, and the correct way is to say: *rakana yarkunu*—like *nasara*—and *rakina yarkanu*—like *alima*. The Exalted said: **وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا** — **Do not lean toward the wrongdoers**. And "a she-camel with a well-formed udder" (ناقة مركنة الضرع) has "pillars" (أركان) that make it large. The *mirkan* is the wash-basin. The "pillars of worship" (أركان العبادات) are its sides upon which its structure is built, and by leaving them, it becomes void. [End of quote]. This is close to what was mentioned in *al-Misbah*.

The truth is that it is relying (الاعتماد) on a thing out of an inclination (ميل) toward it, not mere reliance alone. For this reason, it is made transitive with "toward" (إلى) rather than "upon" (على). What the lexicographers mentioned is an explanation of it by what is more general than its meaning, as is their habit.

Thus, "leaning toward the wrongdoers" (الركون إلى الذين ظلموا) is a type of reliance upon them out of an inclination toward them, either in the religion itself—such as mentioning some of its truths in a way that they benefit from it, or overlooking some of its truths whose disclosure would harm them—or in the religious life, such as allowing them a type of involvement in managing the affairs of the religious society through the guardianship of public affairs (ولاية الأمور العامة), or the affection (المودة) that leads to social mixing and influence in the vital affairs of the society or the individual.

In summary, approaching the wrongdoers in the matter of religion or religious life with a type of reliance and dependence (الاتكاء) removes the religion or religious life from independence in influence and changes them from their pure direction. The necessary consequence of this is seeking the truth through the path of falsehood, or reviving a right by reviving a wrong, and ultimately, killing the truth in order to revive it.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is that the Exalted, in His address in this verse—which is a completion of the address in the previous verse—combined the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers of his nation. The affairs belonging to him and his nation are the religious gnosis (المعارف الدينية), ethics, and Islamic traditions (السنن الإسلامية) in their propagation, preservation, and implementation, as well as the social life that accords with them, and the guardianship of the affairs of the Islamic society, and the individual's identification with the religion and his following the tradition of religious life. Thus, it is not for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) nor for his nation to lean in any of that toward the wrongdoers.

Furthermore, it is known that these two verses are like a conclusion drawn from the stories of the wrongdoing towns whom Allah seized for their wrongdoing. They are derived from them and look toward them. The wrongdoing of these destroyed nations was not only in their polytheism in Allah the Exalted and the worship of idols; rather, among their deeds that Allah censured was the following of wrongdoers and corruption on earth after its reformation—which is the following of the wrongdoing traditions established by tyrannical rulers, which the people then follow, thereby becoming wrongdoers.

It is also known from the context that the two verses are ordered in their purpose: the first prohibits them from being among those who did wrong, and the second prohibits them from approaching them, inclining toward them, and relying (1) in their right upon their falsehood. Thus, His saying, "Do not lean toward the wrongdoers" (لا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا), is a prohibition of inclining toward them, relying on them, and building upon their falsehood in the matter of the origin of religion and religious life altogether.

The occurrence of the two verses in the position of a conclusion derived from the aforementioned stories indicates that what is meant by "those who did wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) in the verse is not anyone from whom any wrongdoing whatsoever has occurred. Otherwise, it would encompass all people except the people of infallibility (أهل العصمة), and the prohibition would then have no meaning. Nor is what is meant by "those who did wrong" the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين)—meaning those characterized by this description and persisting in their wrongdoing—for giving the verb, which indicates mere occurrence, the meaning of the attribute, which indicates characterization and persistence, requires reasons, none of which are present here, and nothing indicates something else arbitrarily.

Rather, what is meant by "those who did wrong" are people whose state in wrongdoing is like the state of those whom Allah narrated in the previous verses from the destroyed nations. The situation in their story was that the Exalted took the people as a whole in the face of the divine call directed toward them, then divided them into those who accepted it and those who rejected it. He then referred to those who accepted it as "those who believed" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) in several places in the mentioned stories, and to those who rejected it as "those who did wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) and similar terms in more than ten places, such as His saying: **وَلا تُخاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا** — **and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers** (11:37), and His saying: **وَأَخَذَتِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ** — **and the Cry seized those who were wrongdoers** (11:67), and His saying: **وَتِلْكَ عادٌ جَحَدُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا رُسُلَهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَمْرَ كُلِّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ** — **Such was ‘Ad: they denied the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles, and followed the bid of every obstinate tyrant.** (11:59), and His saying: **أَلا إِنَّ ثَمُودَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّهُمْ** — **Behold! Thamud indeed disbelieved in their Lord** (11:68), and His saying: **أَلا بُعْداً لِمَدْيَنَ كَما بَعِدَتْ ثَمُودُ** — **Behold! Away with Midian, just as Thamud were done away with!** (11:95), among others.

The Glorified expressed their rejection and acceptance in the face of the divine call and in relation to it using the past tense (الفعل الماضي), which indicates mere occurrence and happening. As for outside the station of comparison and relation, the expression is by the attribute (الصفة), such as His saying: **وَقِيلَ بُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ** — **and it was said, ‘Away with the wrongdoing lot!’** (11:44), and His saying: **وَما هِيَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — **and it is not far from the wrongdoers** (11:83), and His saying: **وَلا تَتَوَلَّوْا مُجْرِمِينَ** — **and do not turn away as guilty ones** (11:52), and so on, which is frequent.

The requirement of the station of comparison and relation to the call—in terms of acceptance and rejection—is to suffice with mere happening and occurrence, and to show that they were marked with one of the two marks: faith (الإيمان) or wrongdoing (الظلم). There is no need to mention characterization and persistence in the state. Thus, the meaning of His saying, "they did wrong," "they disobeyed," and "they followed the bid of Pharaoh," is that they were marked with the mark of wrongdoing, disobedience, and following the bid of Pharaoh. And the meaning of "We delivered those who believed" is: We delivered those who were marked with the mark of faith and identified by its sign.

Thus, the expression in the past tense was sufficient to convey the origin of the mentioned marking, even if it is something that characterization follows. In other words, those who did wrong from the people of Noah became wrongdoers thereby, but the concern—according to the station—was attached to the occurrence of wrongdoing from them, not to its becoming an inseparable attribute for them thereafter. Therefore, you see that whenever the discourse exits the station of comparison and relation in some way, it returns to the expression by the attribute, such as His saying: "Away with the wrongdoing lot!" and His saying: "and do not be with the disbelievers." So understand that.

From here, what is in the people's interpretation of His saying, "those who did wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا), becomes clear—where some took it as indicating the mere occurrence of some wrongdoing, and others as meaning the attribute, i.e., "do not lean toward the wrongdoers." The author of *al-Manar* said in his exegesis: Al-Zamakhshari interpreted "those who did wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) by saying: "that is, toward those from whom wrongdoing was found," and he did not say: "toward the wrongdoers." It is narrated that al-Muwaffaq prayed behind the Imam, who read this verse, and he fainted. When he recovered, he was asked [about it], and he said: "This is regarding one who leaned toward a wrong; so what about the wrongdoer?" [End of quote].

The meaning of this is that the threat in the verse includes one who inclines even slightly toward one from whom a little wrongdoing has occurred—any wrongdoing whatsoever. This is also an error. Rather, what is meant by "those who did wrong" in the verse is the group of wrongdoers from the enemies of the believers who harm them and tempt them away from their religion—from the polytheists—to turn them back from it. They are like "those who disbelieved" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) in the many verses where the group of disbelievers is meant, not every individual from whom disbelief occurred in the past. Sufficient for you is His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — **As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith.** (2:6). Those addressed by the prohibition are those addressed in the previous verse by His saying: "So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you."

These polytheist enemies were referred to as "those who did wrong," just as the nations of the first messengers were referred to in their stories in this Surah in verses 37, 67, and 94. They were also referred to therein as "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين), such as His saying: "Away with the wrongdoing lot!" Thus, there is no difference in these verses between the expression by the attribute and the expression by the relative pronoun and its clause, for they are one and the same in the discourse about the nations. [End of quote].

As for the saying of the author of *al-Kashshaf*: "What is meant by 'those who did wrong' are those from whom wrongdoing was found," and he did not say: "toward the wrongdoers"—this is so, but it does not benefit him. For the expression by the verb in the station we have established, even if it does not convey more than the occurrence of the event-meaning (المعنى الحدثي), it applies to the meaning of the attribute, as in His saying: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى وَآثَرَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا فَإِنَّ الْجَحِيمَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى وَأَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَنَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى فَإِنَّ الْجَنَّةَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى** — **As for him who was rebellious and preferred the life of this world, his abode will indeed be hell. But as for him who was afraid of standing before his Lord and forbade the soul from [following] desire, his abode will indeed be paradise.** (79:37-41), where He expressed it by the verb, yet it applies to the meaning of the attribute.

As for the saying of the author of *al-Manar*: "What is meant by 'those who did wrong' in the verse is the group of wrongdoers from the enemies of the believers who harm them and tempt them away from their religion—from the polytheists—to turn them back from it"—this is pure arbitrariness. What evidence indicates the verse's inability to include the wrongdoers from the People of the Book, when Allah mentioned them finally in the company of the wrongdoers due to their disagreement in the Book of Allah out of envy? Allah has prohibited their guardianship (ولاية) and was severe in it, even saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ** — **Any of you who takes them as friends is indeed one of them.** (5:51). And He said regarding the guardianship of disbelievers in general: **وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ اللهِ فِي شَيْءٍ** — **and whoever does that has nothing to do with Allah** (3:28).

And what barrier prevents the verse from including the wrongdoers from this nation, among whom are those more wretched than the tyrants of 'Ad and Thamud, and more rebellious than Pharaoh, Haman, and Korah?

The mere fact that Islam, at the time of the Surah's revelation, was afflicted by Quraysh and the polytheists of Mecca and its surroundings does not necessitate a restriction in the wording. For the specificity of the occasion (خصوص المورد) does not restrict the generality of the wording (عموم اللفظ). Thus, the verse prohibits leaning toward anyone marked with the mark of wrongdoing, whether he be a polytheist or a monotheist, a Muslim or from the People of the Book.

As for his saying: "What is meant by His saying, 'As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith,' is the attributive meaning even if it is a verb—i.e., the disbelievers—not every individual from whom disbelief occurred in the past"—therein is that we explained in the discourse on that verse and in other places previously in the book that the verse is specific to the disbelievers from the leaders of Quraysh who opposed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at the beginning of his affair until Allah sufficed him against them and destroyed them at Badr and elsewhere, and that they are the ones named "those who disbelieved" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). The verse is not general. If the verse were general and what was meant by "those who disbelieved" were the disbelievers [as a class], as he said, the verse would not be true in what it informs. For how many disbelievers have believed, even from the disbelievers of Mecca after the revelation of this verse!

If it were said: "What is meant by them are those who did not believe until the end of their lives," the verse would not convey anything, as there is no meaning to our saying: "Indeed, the disbelievers who do not believe until the end of their lives do not believe." Thus, there is no escape from taking it as a specific verse, not a general one, and that what is meant by "those who disbelieved" is a specific group by their persons.

We have mentioned previously that "those who believed" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) is also—where there is no evidence in the context indicating otherwise—an honorific name for those who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at the beginning of his true call, and to them the addresses of "O you who have faith" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) in the Quran are specific, even if others share with them in the rulings.

As for his saying at the end of his discourse: "These polytheist enemies were referred to as 'those who did wrong,' just as the nations of the first messengers were referred to in their stories in this Surah, and they were also referred to therein as 'the wrongdoers'; thus there is no difference between the two expressions," etc.—its summary is that referring to them sometimes as "those who did wrong" and sometimes as "the wrongdoers" is evidence that what is meant by the two words is one.

Therein is that the aspect of linguistic concern (العناية الكلامية) which we previously alluded to was hidden from him, and the unity of the referent (المصداق) of the two words does not necessitate the unity of the concern attached to them.

Thus, the summary of the verse's content is the prohibition of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his nation from leaning toward those marked with the mark of wrongdoing by inclining toward them and relying on their wrongdoing in the matter of their religion or their religious life. This is what is meant by His saying: "Do not lean toward the wrongdoers" (وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا).

His saying, "lest the Fire should touch you" (فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ), is a derivation from the leaning; i.e., the consequence of leaning is the touch of the Fire. The consequence of leaning toward the wrongdoing of the people of wrongdoing was made the touch of the Fire, while the consequence of wrongdoing itself is the Fire. This is the difference between approaching wrongdoing and being characterized by wrongdoing itself.

His saying, "and you will have no protectors besides Allah" (وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ), is in the position of a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the object of "should touch you" (فَتَمَسَّكُمُ); i.e., the Fire will touch you in a state where you have no protectors besides Allah, which is the Day of Resurrection when man loses all his protectors besides Allah. Or it is the state of leaning, if what is meant by the Fire is punishment. What is meant by His saying, "then you will not be helped" (ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ), is the denial of intercession according to the first [interpretation] and divine abandonment (الخذلان) according to the second.

The expression with "then" (ثُمَّ) in His saying, "then you will not be helped," is to indicate the conclusion of the matter upon that with disappointment and abandonment. It is as if it were said: the Fire will touch you and you have none but Allah, so you call upon Him and He does not answer you, and you seek His help and He does not help you; thus your affair ends in loss, disappointment, and abandonment.

From the preceding discussions on the verse, several matters have been obtained:

First: That which is prohibited in the verse is only leaning toward the people of wrongdoing in the matter of religion or religious life—such as remaining silent in the exposition of religious truths regarding matters that harm them, or leaving an action they do not approve of, or appointing them over the society and entrusting them with public affairs, or conducting religious matters by their hands and power, and the like.

As for leaning and relying on them in social interaction or transactions such as buying and selling, and trusting them and entrusting them in some matters, all of that is not included in the prohibition in the verse. For it is not a leaning in religion or religious life. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), when he left Mecca at night for the cave, trusted a polytheist man from whom he hired a mount for the journey and entrusted him to meet him with it at the cave after three days. He, as well as the Muslims, used to deal with the disbelievers and polytheists within his sight and hearing.

Second: The leaning prohibited in the verse is more specific than the guardianship (الولاية) prohibited in many other verses. For **guardianship** (الولاية) — *the act of taking as a close ally or protector* — is approaching them such that it makes the Muslims subject to being influenced by their religion, ethics, or the wrongdoing traditions prevalent in their societies, while they are enemies of the religion. As for leaning toward them, it is building the religion or religious life upon their wrongdoing. Thus, it is more specific than guardianship in its application; i.e., every instance where there is leaning, there is guardianship, but not vice versa. The manifestation of the effect in leaning is actual, while in guardianship it is more general than being actual.

It appears from a group of commentators that the leaning prohibited in the verse is the guardianship prohibited in other verses.

The author of *al-Manar* said in his exegesis, after quoting from al-Zamakhshari his saying: "The prohibition in the verse encompasses descending into their desires, being devoted to them, accompanying them, sitting with them, visiting them, compromising (المداهنة) with them, being pleased with their deeds, imitating them, adopting their dress, casting one's eyes toward their splendor, and mentioning them with that which contains veneration for them. And ponder His saying: 'Do not lean' (وَلا تَرْكَنُوا), for leaning is a slight inclination, and His saying: 'toward those who did wrong' (إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا), i.e., toward those from whom wrongdoing was found, and He did not say: 'toward the wrongdoers.'" [End of quote].

I say: Everything he inserted into the prohibition of leaning toward those who did wrong is ugly in itself and not befitting for a believer to commit. It may be among the minor consequences of leaning, but it is not correct to make any of it an interpretation of the verse intended by it. The first one addressed by it is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the foremost ones (السابقون الأولون) to repentance from polytheism and faith with him. None of them were suspected of being devoted to the wrongdoing polytheists, descending into their desires, or being pleased with their deeds... until the end of what he elaborated upon.

He contradicted himself therein: first, by admitting that some of the mentioned matters are consequences of leaning, yet he belittled them and denied the verse's inclusion of them. All disobedience is great and not to be underestimated, but this is the habit of most commentators—they do not shrink from attributing some laxity to His exposition.

More corrupt than that is his saying: "The first one addressed by this prohibition is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the foremost ones, and they were not suspected of being devoted to the wrongdoing polytheists, descending into their desires, or being pleased with their deeds," etc. For therein is, first: that the address is a single address directed toward him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and toward his nation; there is no "first" or "second" in it. The temporal precedence of some of those addressed over others does not necessitate the address's inability to include some of what is in the later ones that was not in the earlier ones, if the wording encompasses it.

Second: That the addressee's not being suspected of disobedience does not prevent directing the prohibition toward him, especially the prohibitions issued from the station of legislation (التشريع). It only prevents its emphasis and insistence. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was prohibited from what is greater than leaning toward those who did wrong, such as polytheism in Allah and leaving the propagation of some of His commands and prohibitions. The Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ** — **Certainly it has been revealed to you and to those [who were] before you: ‘If you ascribe a partner [to Allah], your charitable deeds will surely go for nothing, and you will surely be among the losers.’** (39:65). And He said: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ** — **O Apostle! Deliver that which has been sent down to you from your Lord, for if you do not, you will not have delivered His message.** (5:67). And He said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللهَ وَلا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ وَالْمُنافِقِينَ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً وَاتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — **O Prophet! Be wary of Allah and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. Follow what is revealed to you from your Lord.** (33:1-2). It is never suspected of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he would associate partners with his Lord, nor that he would not deliver what was sent down to him from his Lord, or obey the disbelievers and hypocrites, or not follow what was revealed to him from his Lord, among other prohibitions.

Likewise, the foremost ones were prohibited from matters greater than the mentioned leaning or similar to it, such as His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً** — **And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively** (8:25). It was revealed regarding the people of Badr, among whom were the foremost ones, and some of them were described by His saying: "those who did wrong" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا), which is a harsher tone than His saying: "Do not lean toward the wrongdoers." This is like several prohibitions appearing in His speech regarding the stories of Badr, Uhud, and Hunayn, and the prohibitions directed toward the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). All of that was not suspected of the foremost ones to be afflicted with, yet some of them were afflicted with some of it later.

Third: That the verse, by its context supported by the indication of the station, only prohibits leaning toward those who did wrong in that wherein they are wrongdoers—that is, the Muslims building their true religion or their religious life upon something of their wrongdoing, which is to observe, in their true speech and true action, the side of their wrongdoing and falsehood, such that there is a revival of the right by means of reviving the wrong. Its end is the revival of a right by the killing of another right, as previously alluded to.

As for inclining toward something of their wrongdoing and introducing it into the religion, or implementing it in the Islamic society or in the context of personal life, it is not from leaning toward the wrongdoers; rather, it is entering into the group of the wrongdoers.

This matter has been confused for many commentators, so they brought forth in this station discussions that do not touch the verse in the slightest. We have overlooked mentioning them and discussing their correctness or error, preferring brevity. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to their exegeses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ** — **Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night. Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds.** (11:114) etc. The "two ends of the day" (طرفا النهار) are the morning and the evening. "Early hours" (الزلف) is the plural of *zulfa*, like *qurab* is the plural of *qurba* in wording and meaning according to what is said. It is an attribute that stands in place of its described noun, such as "hours" and the like. The estimation is: "and hours of the night" (وساعات من الليل) that are closer to the day.

The meaning is: maintain the prayer in the morning and the evening, and in hours of the night that are closer to the day. From the five daily prayers, this applies to the Dawn (الصبح) and Afternoon (العصر) prayers—which is the evening prayer—and the Sunset (المغرب) and Night (العشاء الآخرة) prayers, their time being "early hours of the night" (زلف من الليل), as said by some. Or to the Dawn and Sunset prayers—their time being the two ends of the day—and the Night prayer, its time being "early hours of the night," as said by others. Other things have also been said.

However, since the discussion is jurisprudential (فقهيا), what is to be followed therein is what has been reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Imams of his household (عليه السلام) of exposition. It will come in the forthcoming traditional discourse, Allah willing.

His saying, "Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds" (إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ), is a justification for His saying, "Maintain the prayer" (وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ), and an exposition that the prayers are "good deeds" (حسنات) arriving upon the souls of the believers, removing the traces of acts of disobedience, which are the "misdeeds" (السيئات) that afflict them. A discussion on this subject has preceded in the issue of "nullification of deeds" (الحبط) in the second volume of this book.

His saying, "That is an admonition for the mindful" (ذلِكَ ذِكْرى لِلذَّاكِرِينَ), means: this which has been mentioned—that good deeds efface misdeeds—despite its high status, is a reminder for those characterized by the remembrance (ذكر) of Allah the Exalted among His servants.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — **And be patient; for Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous.** (11:115). Then He commanded him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with patience (الصبر) after He commanded him with prayer, just as He combined them in His saying: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلاةِ** — **And take recourse in patience and prayer** (2:45). This is because each of them, in its domain, is among the greatest pillars—that is, prayer in worship (العبادات), and patience in ethics (الأخلاق). The Exalted said regarding prayer: **وَلَذِكْرُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ** — **and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater** (29:45), and He said regarding patience: **إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** — **that is indeed the course of positive action** (42:43).

Their combination is the best means by which to seek help against calamities and hardships. Patience preserves the soul from anxiety, grief, and defeat, while prayer directs it toward the side of the Lord the Exalted, so it forgets the hardships it encounters. An exposition of that has preceded in the exegesis of verse 45 of Surah al-Baqarah in the first volume of the book.

The absolute nature of the command for patience indicates that what is meant by it is more general than patience in worship, patience against disobedience, and patience during calamity. Based on this, it would be a command for patience regarding all the preceding commands and prohibitions—namely His saying: "So be steadfast," "and do not transgress," "and do not lean," and "Maintain the prayer."

However, singling out the command and specifying it for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) indicates that it is patience in a matter specific to him. Otherwise, it would have been said: "And be [all of you] patient" (واصبروا), following the context. This supports the view of those who said: What is meant is: be patient with the harm of your people in the path of your call to Allah the Glorified and the wrongdoing of the wrongdoers among them. As for His saying, "Maintain the prayer," it is not a command for what is specific to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of prayer; rather, it is a command for his maintaining the prayer with those who follow him from the believers in congregation (جماعة). Thus, it is a command for all of them to pray; so understand that.

His saying, "for Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous" (فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ), is a justification for the command for patience.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَوْ لا كانَ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ أُولُوا بَقِيَّةٍ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْفَسادِ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **Why were there not among the generations before you any persons of merit who would forbid corruption on the earth** (11:116) etc. *Lawlā* (لو لا) is in the sense of *hallā* (هلا) [why not], and it conveys wonder and rebuke. The meaning is: Why were there not among the generations (القرون) that were before you—whom We destroyed with punishment and annihilation—"persons of merit" (أُولُوا بَقِيَّةٍ), i.e., a remaining people who would forbid corruption on the earth to reform it thereby and preserve their nation from extirpation?

His saying, "except for a few of those whom We delivered from among them" (إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّنْ أَنْجَيْنا مِنْهُمْ), is an exception from the sense of negation in the previous sentence. For the meaning is: It is a wonder that there were not among the past generations—despite what they saw of Allah’s signs and witnessed of His punishment—remnants who would forbid corruption on the earth, except for a few of those whom We delivered from punishment and destruction among them, for they used to forbid corruption.

His saying, "But the wrongdoers pursued what they had been pampered in, and they were guilty" (وَاتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ما أُتْرِفُوا فِيهِ وَكانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ), is an exposition of the state of the rest of them after the exception—and they were the majority. He identified them as "those who did wrong" and explained that they followed the pleasures of the world in which they were pampered (أترفوا) and were guilty (مجرمين).

Through this exception and this remainder whose state was mentioned, the division of people into two classes has been obtained—

...differing: those who are saved by Allah’s deliverance and the criminals. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: **وَلَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ** "Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community; but they continue to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy." (11:118-119).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ الْقُرى بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُها مُصْلِحُونَ** "Your Lord would never destroy the towns unjustly while their people were righteous." (11:117). That is, it has never been His divine custom (السنة) to destroy towns whose inhabitants are righteous (مصلحون), because that would be injustice (ظلم), and your Lord does not wrong anyone. Thus, His saying "unjustly" (بظلم) is an explanatory qualification (قيد توضيحي), not a restrictive one (احترازي); it signifies that His custom is not to destroy righteous towns because doing so would constitute injustice, and **وَما رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ** "and your Lord is not at all unjust to [His] servants." (41:46).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ** — up to His saying — **أَجْمَعِينَ** "Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community; but they continue to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy... all together." (11:118-119). The word "succession" (الخلف) refers to that which comes after the preceding, and it is the root for the various derivatives of this linguistic stem. It is said: "He succeeded (خلف) his father," meaning he took his place because he came after him. "He broke (أخلف) his promise," meaning he did not fulfill it, as if he cast it behind him. "He died and left (خلف) a son," meaning he left him behind. "He appointed someone as a successor (استخلف)," meaning he requested that the person act as a deputy for him after his absence or death, or by way of a specific divine appointment, such as Allah’s appointment of Adam and his progeny as vicegerents (استخلاف) on the earth. "So-and-so opposed (خالف) so-and-so" or "they disagreed (تخالفا)" when they diverge in opinion or action, as if each of them puts the other behind him. "He fell behind (تخلف) his command" when he turned away and did not comply. "The people differed (اختلف) regarding such-and-such" when some opposed others, thereby putting the other's view behind them. "The people frequented (اختلف) so-and-so" when they entered upon him one after another, and "so-and-so visited (اختلف) so-and-so" when he entered upon him repeatedly, one time after another.

Furthermore, difference (الاختلاف) — the opposite of which is agreement (الاتفاق) — is among the matters that a sound nature (الطبع السليم) does not approve of, due to the resulting dissipation and weakening of forces, and other unpraiseworthy effects such as dispute, quarreling, argumentation, fighting, and discord; all of which strip away security and peace. However, there is a type of difference that is unavoidable in the human world, which is the difference arising from natural dispositions (الطبائع) that lead to differences in physical constitutions (البنى). Physical compositions differ among individuals, which leads to differences in physical and spiritual aptitudes (الاستعدادات). When the differences of environments and circumstances are added to this, there appear differences in tastes, customs, manners, objectives, and specific or personal actions within human societies. Social research has clarified that were it not for this, human society would not survive for the blink of an eye.

Allah, the Exalted, has mentioned this in His Book and attributed it to Himself, where He said: **نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَرَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا** "It is We who dispense their livelihood between them in the life of the world, and raise some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service." (43:32). He, the Exalted, has not censured this in any part of His speech except when it is accompanied by personal whim (هوى النفس) and opposes the guidance of intellect (عقل).

Difference in religion (الدين) is not of this [unavoidable] type, for Allah, the Glorified, mentions that He created mankind upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — of knowing Him and His monotheism (التوحيد). He proportioned the human soul and inspired it with its vice and its godwariness (تقواها), and the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) is part of that primordial nature upon which He created mankind; there is no altering the creation of Allah. Therefore, in various places in His speech, He attributes difference in religion to the rebellion (بغي) and injustice (ظلم) of those who differ: **فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ** "And they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves." (45:17).

Allah has combined these two types of difference in His saying: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** "Mankind were but one community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213) — and this is the first difference, regarding life and livelihood — **وَما اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ** "And none differed in it" — and this is the second difference, regarding religion — **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ فَهَدَى اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ** "except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Then Allah guided those who had faith to the truth of what they differed about, by His leave." (2:213). This is what His speech, the Exalted, provides regarding the meaning of difference (الاختلاف).

That which He mentioned in His saying: **وَلَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً** "Had your Lord wished, He would have made mankind one community" (11:118) refers to the removal of difference from among them and uniting them upon a single word upon which they agree. It is evident that this refers to what He, the Exalted, mentioned in the verses preceding this one regarding their difference in the matter of religion and their division into a group whom Allah saved — and they are few — and another group who were the wrongdoers (الظالمين).

The meaning, then, is that although they differed in religion, they did not thereby frustrate Allah; had He wished, He would have made mankind one community that does not differ in religion. This is similar to His saying: **وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** "With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path, and some of them are devious. Had He wished, He would have guided you all." (16:9), and His saying: **أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً** "Have not the faithful yet known that had Allah wished He would have guided mankind all together?" (13:31).

Accordingly, His saying: **وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ** "but they continue to differ" (11:118) refers solely to difference in religion, for that is what He informs us He would have removed from among them had He so wished. The discourse implies: Had Allah wished, He would have removed the difference from among them, but He did not wish that, so they are perpetually differing.

Furthermore, His saying: **إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ** "except those on whom your Lord has mercy" (11:119) explicitly states that He has removed it from a group upon whom He had mercy. As for difference in matters other than religion, Allah, the Exalted, has not removed it even from the group who received mercy; He only removed from them the religious difference which He censures and attributes to rebellion (البغي) after the knowledge of the truth.

His saying: **إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ** "except those on whom your Lord has mercy" is an exception (استثناء) from His saying: **وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ** "but they continue to differ." That is, people will always oppose one another regarding the truth, except those upon whom Allah has had mercy, for they do not differ regarding the truth nor do they diverge from it. **Mercy** (الرحمة) here is divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية), as indicated by His saying: **فَهَدَى اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ** "Then Allah guided those who had faith to the truth of what they differed about, by His leave." (2:213).

If you were to say: The meaning of people's "differing" is that some of them oppose others through negation and affirmation; thus, the meaning of His saying "but they continue to differ" would be that they are perpetually divided into those on the truth and those on falsehood. In that case, the exception would not be valid except in terms of time, not individuals. This is because adding the phrase "except those on whom your Lord has mercy" would make the meaning: "They are perpetually divided into those on falsehood and those on the truth, except those upon whom your Lord has mercy, for they are not divided into two groups, but are only on the truth." It is known that those excepted are the ones on the truth, so the meaning of the discourse would revert to: "Among them are those on falsehood and those on the truth, and those on the truth are on the truth, with no falsehood among them." Such a statement would be redundant and without benefit.

Moreover, there would be no sense in excepting those on the truth from the ruling of "difference" at all, as they are among the people who are differing, and the state of difference is established by them and those on falsehood together.

I reply: The "difference" mentioned in this verse and other verses addressing it and censuring its people is specifically the difference regarding the truth and the opposition of some to others concerning the truth. Even if this necessitates that some of them are upon the truth and possess insight (بصيرة) into the matter, when it is attributed to the whole — that is, the society — its necessary consequence is the society's doubt and their divergence from the truth, their failure to unite upon it, and their leaving it aside. Its implication is the concealment of the truth from them and their falling into doubt regarding it.

Allah, the Glorified, only censures difference because of this necessary consequence, which is division and turning away from the truth. The verses bear witness to this, for He, the Exalted, censures all those who differ because of their differing, not just those on falsehood among them. Were it not that "those who differ" refers to the proponents of differing opinions or actions that divide them from the truth, this [general censure] would not be correct.

Among the best supports for this is the saying of the Exalted: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus: ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13), where He expressed difference as "division" (التفرق). Likewise is His saying: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** "‘This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow other ways, for they will separate you from His way.’" (6:153). This is even clearer in its indication than the previous one, as it places the people of truth who adhere to His way outside the category of the people of division and difference.

For this reason, you see that in most instances where He mentions their difference in the Book, He follows it with their doubt (ارتياب) concerning it, such as His saying in the verses mentioned previously: **وَلَقَد آتَينا موسَى الكِتابَ فَاختُلِفَ فيهِ وَلَولا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَت مِن رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَينَهُم وَإِنَّهُم لَفي شَكٍّ مِنهُ مُريبٍ** "Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it; and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them. Indeed they are in a grave doubt concerning it." (11:110). He has repeated this meaning in several places in His speech.

The Exalted also said: **عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ** "What is it that they are questioning each other about? About the great news, concerning which they differ." (78:1-3). That is, each of them brings a statement that distances them from the truth, so they divide. And He said: **إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ** "Indeed you are in a discordant view. He who is perverted is perverted from it. Perish the liars!" (51:7-10). That is, a view that does not stand upon a single face nor is it built upon knowledge; rather, it is conjecture (الخرص) and opinion that has produced it among you.

In this same vein is the saying of the Exalted: **يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَأَنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "O People of the Book! Why do you confound the truth with falsehood, and conceal the truth while you know [it]?" (3:71). This censured confounding (اللبس) on their part was by manifesting a statement that resembles the truth but is not; it is the casting of division by which the truth is hidden.

Thus, the intended meaning of their "difference" is their invention of statements and opinions by which they diverge from the truth and through which doubt appears. Because of their following of whims that oppose the truth, they manifest their false opinions in various forms that simulate the form of truth, in order to veil it from people's understanding out of rebellion and enmity after they had known the truth. Thus, it is their difference regarding the truth after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves.

It becomes clear from what has preceded at length that the indication in His saying: **وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ** "and for that He created them" (11:119) is to the "mercy" (الرحمة) indicated by His saying: "except those on whom your Lord has mercy." The grammatical femininity of the word "mercy" (الرحمة) does not contradict the masculinity of the demonstrative pronoun (ذلك) because the verbal noun (المصدر) allows for both genders. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللهِ قَرِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "Indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous." (7:56). This is because you have realized that this difference is a rebellion on their part that divides them from the truth, conceals it, and manifests falsehood. It is not permissible for falsehood to be a true objective (غاية حقيقية) for the Truth, the Exalted, in His creation. There is no sense in Allah, the Glorified, bringing the human world into existence so that they may rebel, kill the truth, and revive falsehood, only for Him to destroy them and then punish them with an eternal fire. The Noble Quran refutes this with all its explanations.

Furthermore, the context of the verses — even disregarding what has been mentioned — refutes that [other interpretation]. For they are in the position of explaining that Allah, the Exalted, calls people through His kindness and mercy to that which contains their good and happiness, without wanting any injustice or evil for them. However, through their injustice and their difference regarding the truth, they disdain His call, deny His signs, worship other than Him, and spread corruption on the earth, thereby deserving punishment. "Your Lord would never destroy the towns unjustly while their people were righteous," nor would He create them to rebel and corrupt so that He might destroy them. Thus, that which is from Him is mercy and guidance, while that which results from their rebellion, difference, and injustice returns to themselves. This is what the context of the verses provides.
